patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch is part of a larger series that aims to improve memory management in the Linux kernel. Specifically, it uses the folio_zero_user function for user pages in the post_alloc_hook. The goal is to make the code more efficient and easier to understand by breaking down complex changes into smaller, more manageable pieces. The patch set has become unwieldy due to its complexity and scope, so Gregory suggests splitting it up into smaller pull-ahead sets that can be reviewed separately. This patch is one of the core improvements that will eventually lead to a final feature in multiple kernel versions.

review_comments.author: Michael Tsirkin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Michael Tsirkin expressed concern that the API is unclear and lacks a unified vision, making it difficult for non-mm maintainers like himself to understand what changes are intended down the road.
review_comments.raw_body:
What I worry about is people can't agree what api they want.

Simply not being an mm maintainer, I don't really have the
perspective of what changes are envisioned down the road
and so what api makes sense for you guys.

I don't mind trying all kind of approaches, but it seems to
be past the point where people feel it's costing too much of
their time with all of these revisions.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author acknowledges that the patch set has become complex due to multiple requests for changes, and suggests breaking it up into smaller pull-ahead sets to make review easier. They propose starting a discussion thread to lay out needs and wants, and explain why certain APIs are necessary.

Author acknowledges that the patch set is complex and has become unwieldy, agrees that breaking it up into smaller sets would help clarify the confusion, and suggests starting a discussion thread to gather opinions before revising.
review_comments.raw_body:
Oh that's just our base state of existence.  We mostly agree that
all APIs are bad in some way and we don't want any of them :P

What you're looking for is to get people to agree to the
least-offensive, least-worst option :]

I don't think we're far off from that.  I suggest doing as Zi said and
start a [DISCUSSION] thread on specifically this and lay out the needs
and wants and design issues that you've learned from the past set of
versions and continue the discussion there.

It helps to take some snippets from your set to lay out what you've
learned and explain why you need the folio_user_zero() stuff to get from
A->Z, and then let maintainers hash out whether that should live in
post_alloc_hook or new interfaces (or outside page_alloc.c altogether).

---

People are still commenting, so I don't think you've gotten there yet.
I think the rate of revision is what's costing too much attention.

You'd save yourself some revisions by taking the attention you have
right now and starting the discussion thread (and consider submitting
the topic to LPC if that's something interests you!).

All this is to say you're doing fine, just keep on keepin' on. Maybe
pivot your approach from iterations to discussion for a bit until the
opinions settle.

~Gregory

Specifically, it uses the folio_zero_user function for user pages in the post_alloc_hook. The goal is to make the code more efficient and easier to understand by breaking down complex changes into smaller, more manageable pieces. review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the churn approach does not prevent developers from passing user_addr = -1 to alloc_frozen(), which can lead to a page being exported to userspace without proper cache flush, and suggested using alloc_page() + vm_insert() as an example of this vulnerability.
review_comments.raw_body:
I agree that misuse can cause trouble. But if we do the churn approach,
what prevents developer from doing alloc_frozen(..., user_addr = -1)
and using the returned page for userspace? It is possible the allocated
page can be exported to userspace later.

BTW, that cache flush thing is fragile even today, you probably can
do alloc_page() + vm_insert() to get a page without doing proper flush
and export it to userspace. There seems to be no mechanism to
prevent that.

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the increased complexity of having two APIs (alloc_frozen and alloc_frozen_user) carries maintenance burden, but did not indicate plans to simplify or restructure in this patch
sentiment_signals: did not commit to addressing the issue
review_comments.raw_body:
Oh of course, I said that elsewhere.  It leaves us in a spot where we're
not technically worse than we were yesterday - except that the surface
of the buddy has increased (developers need to know about 2 APIs instead
of 1).  That carries maintenance burden (if something in alloc_frozen()
changes, something in alloc_frozen_user() may need to change).

There's a careful dance here.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Michael Tsirkin
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch may not work correctly on all architectures, specifically mentioning arm32 where missing the cache flush would result in a performance hit but still produce correct results
review_comments.raw_body:
Probably arch dependent. On arm32, I think if you miss the flush, then
PG_dcache_clean will be clear and then you get a perf hit but
it's still correct. Didn't check others.

review_comments.author: Michael Tsirkin
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that introducing user_addr into the page allocator's operation of flushing the cache is causing unnecessary complexity and inference of safety state, suggesting this might be a fundamental interface issue in mm/, and requested consideration for simplifying or optimizing the zeroing process.

Reviewer questioned whether the patch's handling of GFP_ATOMIC | GFP_ZERO without user_addr introduces a tripping hazard, and suggested that the page allocator should not handle operations involving user_addr cache flush.
review_comments.raw_body:
It's not just the flush, we are also trying to use that to optimize
zeroing.

---

I don't mind discarding all of this and doing something else completely,
but I dislike it that multiple people are apparently now angry that I
don't address all the contradictory comments at the same time.
I thought just sending a patchset to show how the result looks like
is easier than arguing about architecture, and would be helpful.

I'm not pushing any of the mm rework, I was asked to do it,
myself I just want the ridiculously effective optimization in there.


-- 
MST

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that the complexity of the GFP_ATOMIC | GFP_ZERO combination without user_addr is causing issues, but does not explicitly agree to restructure or fix it in a future version.

Author acknowledges that introducing user_addr into the page allocator creates a complexity issue, but does not commit to resolving it or revising the patch.

Author acknowledged that reviewer's concern about subtle complexity and potential tripping hazards in page allocator handling is valid, but emphasized that everyone agrees on the usefulness of the goal.

The author is addressing concerns about the complexity and organization of the patch set, specifically that it has become unwieldy due to multiple requests for changes. The author proposes breaking up the patch set into smaller, more manageable pieces, including a separate patch for the user_addr and zeroing improvement, and suggests creating an RFC document to clarify the goals and scope of the project.
review_comments.raw_body:
I wouldn't say anyone is angry, I think most folks are tripping on the
complexity of the set - which has increased (at the request of others).

---

Such is life in mm/ :] - it's hard to known the entire state machine,
and sometimes the contradictions aren't even wrong.

---

Notice: When folks argue implementation, they largely agree the
end goal is useful.  I haven't seen anyone say your problem isn't
real or that it shouldn't be addressed - just opinions on a particular
path forward (which is utterly normal here).

Getting the right incantation of an API is really hard when the
API being changes is something that underpins the entire kernel.

---

As Lorenzo, David, and Matthew have said, the focus of the patch set
does seem to have become unweildy (in part at the request of folks
asking something be done differently).

What needs to be done now is to break it up into some pull-ahead 
sets that are easier to review.  Having a brief RFC doc that lays out
the set of patches might help clarify the confusion going on here,
especially as new folks come in to ask "What's all this about?".

As a start:

  1) the user_addr and zeroing piece seems like a discrete
     improvement worthy of its own set - aside from end goal.

     This is needed by your patch set, but was requested to
     try to push us towards a more reasonable pattern for
     folio_zero_user().

  2) There are a handful of patches that seem able to pull-ahead
     (some of the mempolicy stuff), either as prep work for #1 or
     just on their own.

     Some of these patches seem like latent bugs that aren't hit by
     current users, but do seem to be doing something subtly wrong?

  3) the final virtio piece seems like it should be entirely separate
     once the core pieces are done.

It's not uncommon for core changes like this to take multiple prepatory
sets over many major versions before the final feature lands.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David (Arm) expressed strong disagreement with the patch, calling it 'Simple, and hacky -> unmergable', and suggested a different approach where zeroing is moved further out to callers.

Reviewer David (Arm) expressed strong disagreement with the patch, arguing that the existing page/folio zeroing mechanism is a mess and should not be extended with special casing, citing concerns about double-zeroing and the existence of vma_alloc_zeroed_movable_folio() which can be overridden by architectures.

Reviewer David (Arm) expressed frustration that the patch series has not received sufficient discussion or feedback, particularly regarding his initial pushback and Michael's question in RFC v3, which he believes would have been a better time to address concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
Simple, and hacky -> unmergable. I tried to push it into a different (no GFP
flags -> IMHO better) direction, but the patch set grew in complexity.

I kept telling to keep it simple (e.g., no folio_put optimization, no hugetlb
optimization, simple wrapper functions), and ideally we would have gotten a
better discussion with other folks here much earlier.

And I still do not consider providing an user address to selected interfaces
while centralizing zeroing a bad idea. The real question is how that could be
done in a cleaner way.

Or as Willy said, if we could move zeroing further out to callers, where they
can special-case. But given that KASAN and friends interact in their own way
with zeroing doesn't make that super straight forward as people might think.

---

I mean, I would hope that we can agree that our existing page/folio zeroing is a
mess and should not be extended by slapping more special casing on top?

Sure, we can try cleaning it up, but conceptually, zeroing happening at two
places in the callchain, with random optimizations to avoid double-zeroing is
just bad.

The fact that a vma_alloc_zeroed_movable_folio() that can be overridden by
architectures even exists makes me angry. user_alloc_needs_zeroing() is jsut the
tip of the ugly iceberg.

---

There was Willy's comment in RFC v3 [1], which had 19 patches. Unfortunately, he
no longer followed up to my initial push back and Michael's question later [2].

That would have probably been the right time to wait for more discussion.

RFC v4 had 22 patches with little replies.
v5 had 28 patches with little replies.
v6 had 30 patches with no replies.
v7 had 31 patches with little replies.
v8 had 37 patches with no replies.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/aeu5P1bZW3yEH54t@casper.infradead.org/
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/20260426165330-mutt-send-email-mst@kernel.org/

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the folio_zero_user interface should be internal to mm/, agreed to move its logic into vma_alloc_folio, alloc_hugetlb_folio, and alloc_hugetlb_folio_reserve, but noted a catch related to user_alloc_needs_zeroing().
review_comments.raw_body:
Hm, rewinding on this back to v3 here:
https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/016cc5e5-044c-46c6-a668-200f90a64d85@kernel.org/

You said:

  ```
  Exactly, that's why I am saying that vma_alloc_folio() is the only
  external interface people should be using with a user address.
  ```

Going through the list of folio_zero_user references:

Called unconditionally if a folio is acquired:
   fs/hugetlbfs/inode.c:   folio_zero_user(folio, addr);
   mm/hugetlb.c:           folio_zero_user(folio, vmf->real_address);
   mm/memfd.c:             folio_zero_user(folio, 0);

Called when user_alloc_needs_zeroing() and charging passes:
   mm/huge_memory.c:       folio_zero_user(folio, addr);
   mm/memory.c:            folio_zero_user(folio, vmf->address);

No one outside mm/ should know about this interface at all.
Arguably none of these should know about this interface either.

The appropriate place for this logic appears to be:
    vma_alloc_folio
    alloc_hugetlb_folio
    alloc_hugetlb_folio_reserve

The reason to sink it into the post_alloc_hook is to let the buddy
decide whether the page actually needs to be zeroed (like the virtio
situation) based on PG_zeroed or whatever.

It seems like at a minimum moving the logic all the way into
post_alloc_hook lets us actually delete folio_zero_user() as a published
interface and move it entirely within page_alloc.c.

The catch is user_alloc_needs_zeroing() coming along with it.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Zi Yan suggested lifting __GFP_ZERO up in the allocation code so that most of it doesn't need to care about zeroing, and doing the zeroing right before the page is returned to callers.

Reviewer noted that some callers of the page allocator attempt to zero huge pages differently than intended, causing double zeroing issues when init_on_alloc was introduced and mitigated by user_alloc_needs_zeroing().
review_comments.raw_body:
page_alloc.c calling folio_user_zero() is fine, but my question is
whether we should do the zeroing inside post_alloc_hook(), part of
allocation.

What I propose is to lift __GFP_ZERO up as much as possible,
so that most of allocation code does not need to care about it.
We do the zeroing right before the page is returned to callers.

---

Yeah, some callers think they know better about huge page zeroing and want to
do it differently. That caused double zeroing when init_on_alloc was
introduced and was mitigated by user_alloc_needs_zeroing().

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/3] fuse: fix race between registration and connection abortion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) kernel module, specifically in the io_uring code. The issue arises when a connection is being aborted while another thread is registering an SQE (Submission Queue Entry). The patch introduces a check for the connection status under a spinlock, ensuring that if the connection has been aborted, the queue reference count will be decremented and the stop waitqueue will be woken up. This prevents a deadlock situation where the abort/unmount thread hangs indefinitely due to leaked resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/3] fuse: check connection abort during ring creation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a check to ensure that a connection is not aborted while creating a new ring in the fuse_uring_create function. Without this check, a race condition between fuse_uring_create and fuse_chan_abort can lead to unnecessary allocation and teardown of resources, which are eventually cleaned up by fuse_uring_destruct but may linger until process exit even with an aborted connection.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/3] fuse: fix moving cancelled entry to ent_in_userspace list
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation for io_uring, where cancelling an entry would move it to the ent_in_userspace list and cause a crash when checking if the first entry on this list has expired. The fix involves freeing the cancelled entry directly in fuse_uring_cancel() instead of moving it to the ent_in_userspace list, which is safe because cancel is serialized with teardown via queue->lock. Additionally, the patch removes a gate that prevented aborting requests and stopping queues when there were still queued requests, as cancelled entries may have already dropped their references while requests are still pending.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes two issues in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation for io-uring: a race between abort and fuse ring creation/registration, and a null dereference when cancelling entries. The patches address these problems by reordering commit messages, adding reviewed-by tags, and making changes to the code that handle aborting and cancelling requests. Specifically, the patches ensure that request flushing always happens during abort, and remove a gate that prevented request flushing in certain cases.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] fuse: fix EFAULT clobber in fuse_uring_commit
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the fuse_uring_commit function where an EFAULT error is clobbered by a positive residual returned from copy_from_user(). The fix involves assigning err = -EFAULT before jumping to out, ensuring that fuse_uring_req_end() receives a negative errno and sets req->out.h.error to -EFAULT. This prevents FUSE callers from interpreting the positive value as success and proceeding with uninitialized or partial req->out.args.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] fuse: fix data races on ring->ready
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses data races on ring->ready in the fuse kernel module, specifically on weakly-ordered architectures where a CPU may see ring->ready set to true before the corresponding store to fiq->ops is visible. The patch upgrades the store and load operations using smp_store_release() and smp_load_acquire(), respectively, to ensure proper ordering. Additionally, it wraps marked-vs-unmarked accesses in READ_ONCE() to prevent compiler reloads.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Applied the patch without any issues or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 5 Jun 2026 at 21:27, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
>
> On weakly-ordered architectures, the store to fiq->ops can be
> reordered past the store to ring->ready, allowing a CPU that sees
> ring->ready == true via fuse_uring_ready() to dispatch requests
> through a stale fiq->ops pointer. Upgrade the store to
> smp_store_release() and the load in fuse_uring_ready() to
> smp_load_acquire() so that the preceding WRITE_ONCE(fiq->ops, ...)
> is visible to any CPU that observes ring->ready == true.
>
> Additionally, fuse_uring_do_register() publishes ring->ready with
> WRITE_ONCE() but the fast-path check reads it with a plain load.
> This is a marked-vs-unmarked access that KCSAN will flag. Wrap it in
> READ_ONCE() to mark it without adding unnecessary ordering.
>
> Also wrap the fc->ring load in fuse_uring_ready() in READ_ONCE() to
> prevent the compiler from reloading it between the NULL check and the
> dereference.
>
> Fixes: c2c9af9a0b13 ("fuse: Allow to queue fg requests through io-uring")
> Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
> Reviewed-by: Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] fuse: end fuse_req on io-uring cancel task work
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the io-uring implementation for FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) where a request is not properly ended when the task work is cancelled, leading to incorrect behavior and potential stalls. The fix involves calling fuse_uring_req_end() before falling through to fuse_uring_send() in the cancel branch of fuse_uring_send_in_task(). This ensures that the request is correctly ended and its reference is discharged, preventing issues such as repeated cancels inflating a counter and stalling background operations.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch is a response to another patch that attempts to add zero-copy functionality over io-uring for the fuse filesystem. However, it points out that the current implementation has a flaw: when an abort occurs in userspace while a zero-copy request is still in-flight, the kernel will skip the necessary logic due to the absence of a valid command associated with the entry.

review_comments.author: Baokun Li
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Baokun Li noted that when a zero-copy request is in-flight in userspace and an abort occurs, the entry's cmd field will be NULL due to fuse_uring_send() completing the command before the entry enters FRRS_USERSPACE, causing the if (cmd) guard to skip unregistering the buffer table slot. Baokun agreed that introducing complexity for an independent handle to the ring ctx is not worth it and accepted leaving the buffer table slot occupied until the ring is destroyed as a reasonable tradeoff.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

You're right, I missed that. fuse_uring_send() completes the cmd
and sets ent->cmd = NULL before the entry enters FRRS_USERSPACE.
So when abort hits a zero-copied entry, cmd is already gone \u2014 the
if (cmd) guard skips the unregister entirely.

The timeline is:

 1. Request dispatched \u2192 io_buffer_register_bvec(), zero_copied = true
 2. fuse_uring_send() \u2192 ent->cmd = NULL, io_uring_cmd_done()
 3. Server doing zero-copy IO, ent in FRRS_USERSPACE, no cmd
 4. umount -f \u2192 teardown \u2192 cmd == NULL \u2192 unregister never fires

I now understand the problem you described \u2014 to unregister without
cmd, we'd need an independent handle to the ring ctx, and ensuring
its validity is where the complexity lies. I agree that it's not
worth introducing that complexity for this scenario, and leaving the
buffer table slot occupied until the ring is destroyed is a
reasonable tradeoff.

Thanks for taking the time to walk me through this.


Thanks,
Baokun

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the zero-copy request logic doesn't fire for ents in userspace, and agreed to add clarification to the commit message in v4.
review_comments.raw_body:
No worries at all, I think this is very subtle so I will add something
about this to the commit message in v4 to make this more clear.

Thanks,
Joanne
